problems.title: Space Station Oxygen Calculator
problems.description: Commander, you need to calculate oxygen consumption for the crew on the International Space Station. Each astronaut consumes 550 liters of oxygen per day. Given the number of astronauts and the number of days until the next supply ship arrives, calculate the total oxygen needed.

problems.title: Flower Garden Spacing Calculator
problems.description: You're designing a beautiful flower garden. Each flower needs 45cm of space to grow properly. Given the number of flowers you want to plant and the available garden length in centimeters, determine if all flowers will fit and return true or false.

problems.title: Password Strength Validator
problems.description: As a security engineer, you need to validate that user passwords meet the minimum length requirement. Passwords must be at least 8 characters long. Return true if the password is valid, false otherwise.

problems.title: Dragon's Treasure Counter
problems.description: A mighty dragon has a hoard of gold coins, silver coins, and copper coins. Each gold coin is worth 100 silver coins, and each silver coin is worth 100 copper coins. Calculate the total value of the hoard in copper coins.

problems.title: Basketball Game Score
problems.description: You're tracking scores for a basketball team. Regular field goals are worth 2 points, three-pointers are worth 3 points, and free throws are worth 1 point. Calculate the team's total score given the number of each type of shot made.

problems.title: Musical Note Frequency Calculator
problems.description: In music theory, each octave doubles the frequency of a note. Given a base frequency of 440Hz (A4), calculate the frequency of the same note in a different octave. Positive octaves go higher, negative go lower.

problems.title: Art Gallery Frame Spacing
problems.description: You're setting up an art gallery wall that is 12 meters wide. You need to hang an odd number of paintings equally spaced with the center painting in the middle. Calculate the spacing between paintings in meters.

problems.title: Roman Numeral to Number
problems.description: You're an archaeologist deciphering ancient Roman inscriptions. Convert simple Roman numerals (I, V, X, L, C, D, M only) to their numeric values. For this simplified version, assume the numeral is valid and uses additive notation only.

problems.title: Robot Battery Status
problems.description: You're monitoring a Mars rover's battery. The battery has a capacity of 5000 mAh. Given the current charge remaining in mAh, calculate and return the battery percentage as a whole number.

problems.title: Library Late Fee Calculator
problems.description: You're working at a city library. Books have a 14-day loan period. After that, late fees are $0.25 per day. Calculate the total late fee for a book returned a certain number of days late.

problems.title: Bird Migration Distance
problems.description: You're tracking migratory birds. Arctic terns fly from the Arctic to Antarctica and back each year, covering about 71000 km round trip. Calculate how many kilometers a tern has flown after completing a certain number of full migrations.

problems.title: Website Page Load Calculator
problems.description: You're optimizing a website. Each image adds 0.15 seconds to load time, each script adds 0.08 seconds, and CSS files add 0.05 seconds. Calculate the total estimated load time in seconds.

problems.title: Wizard Spell Power Calculator
problems.description: You're a wizard preparing for battle. Your spell power equals your intelligence stat times 3 plus your magic staff's bonus. Calculate the total spell power given intelligence and staff bonus.

problems.title: Marathon Pace Calculator
problems.description: You're training for a marathon. A marathon is 42.195 kilometers. Given your target finishing time in minutes, calculate your required average pace in minutes per kilometer, rounded to 2 decimal places.

problems.title: Guitar String Tuner
problems.description: You're tuning a guitar. Standard tuning frequencies are: E2=82.41Hz, A2=110Hz, D3=146.83Hz, G3=196Hz, B3=246.94Hz, E4=329.63Hz. Given a string name, return its frequency. Return 0 for invalid strings.

problems.title: Paint Color Mixer
problems.description: You're mixing paints for an art project. Red paint costs $12 per liter, blue costs $15 per liter, and yellow costs $10 per liter. Calculate the total cost given the liters of each color needed.

problems.title: Egyptian Pyramid Builder
problems.description: You're an ancient Egyptian architect building a pyramid. Each level of the pyramid uses the square of the level number in blocks (level 1 uses 1 block, level 2 uses 4 blocks, etc.). Calculate total blocks needed for a pyramid with given levels.

problems.title: Spaceship Fuel Efficiency
problems.description: You're piloting a spaceship to Mars. The ship consumes 8.5 kg of fuel per 1000 km traveled. Calculate how much fuel is needed for a journey of a given distance in kilometers.

problems.title: Bakery Discount Calculator
problems.description: You own a bakery offering a 'buy 3 get 1 free' promotion on cookies that cost $2 each. Calculate the total cost for a customer buying a certain number of cookies with the discount applied.

problems.title: Tree Age Calculator
problems.description: You're a forest ranger estimating tree ages. An oak tree grows about 2.5 cm in diameter per year. Given the diameter of an oak tree in centimeters, estimate its age in whole years.

problems.title: Screen Resolution Calculator
problems.description: You're a display technician. Calculate the total number of pixels on a screen given its width and height in pixels. Also determine if it's considered 4K (3840x2160 or more pixels in width).

problems.title: Medieval Tournament Points
problems.description: You're organizing a medieval knight tournament. Knights earn 10 points per jousting win, 5 points per archery hit, and 15 points for melee combat victories. Calculate a knight's total tournament score.

problems.title: Swimming Pool Lap Counter
problems.description: You're training at the pool. An Olympic pool is 50 meters long. Given the number of laps you've completed (one lap = down and back = 100m), calculate the total distance swum in meters.

problems.title: Piano Key Position Finder
problems.description: You're learning piano. A standard piano has 88 keys numbered 1-88 from left to right. Middle C is key 40. Given a key number, determine if it is to the left (lower) or right (higher) of middle C. Return 'left' if lower, 'right' if higher, or 'middle' if it is middle C.

problems.title: Sculpture Material Cost
problems.description: You're commissioning a sculpture. Bronze costs $45 per kg, marble costs $30 per kg, and wood costs $8 per kg. Calculate the material cost for a sculpture given the kilograms of each material used.

problems.title: Mars Colony Supply Calculator
problems.description: You're planning supplies for a Mars colony. Each colonist needs 2.5 kg of food per day. The journey takes 9 months (270 days). Calculate total food needed in kg for a given number of colonists.

problems.title: Taxi Fare Calculator
problems.description: You're driving a taxi. The base fare is $3.50 plus $2.00 per kilometer. Calculate the total fare for a trip of a given distance in kilometers.

problems.title: Beehive Honey Production
problems.description: You're a beekeeper tracking honey production. Each beehive produces about 25 kg of honey per season. Calculate the total honey production for a given number of beehives.

problems.title: WiFi Signal Strength Converter
problems.description: You're a network engineer. WiFi signal strength is measured in dBm. Convert dBm to percentage where -30 dBm = 100% and -90 dBm = 0%. Linearly interpolate between these values.

problems.title: Elf Archery Scoring
problems.description: You're an elf competing in an archery tournament. Bullseye (center) is worth 10 points, inner ring is 8 points, outer ring is 5 points, and miss is 0 points. Calculate total score given hits in each zone.

problems.title: Tennis Set Winner
problems.description: You're umpiring a tennis match. In a set, the first player to win 6 games wins, but must win by 2 games. Given the games won by player 1 and player 2, determine if the set is complete (someone has won).

problems.title: Drum Beat Pattern Generator
problems.description: You're a drummer creating a beat pattern. In 4/4 time, a measure has 4 beats. A kick drum hits on beats 1 and 3, a snare on beats 2 and 4. Given a beat number (1-4), return which drum should play: 'kick', 'snare', or 'rest'.

problems.title: Mosaic Tile Pattern Calculator
problems.description: You're creating a mosaic art piece using square tiles. You want to create a checkerboard pattern where every other tile is a different color. Given the width and height of the mosaic in tiles, calculate how many tiles of the primary color you need (starting with primary color at position 0,0).

problems.title: Code Review Buddy System
problems.description: In a software development team, junior and senior developers are paired for code reviews. Each developer has an experience level, and for optimal knowledge transfer, the absolute difference in their levels should not exceed a maximum threshold. Given two lists of developers, find the maximum number of valid junior-senior pairs that can be formed.

problems.title: Alchemical Ingredient Harmonizer
problems.description: Master alchemists know that potion ingredients must be balanced in pairs of opposing elements (fire-ice, light-dark, earth-air). Given a list of ingredients with their elemental properties, determine if they can all be perfectly paired such that each pair contains complementary elements. The number of each element type matters.

problems.title: Network Traffic Spike Detector
problems.description: A cybersecurity system monitors network packets, recording their sizes in bytes. Anomalies are detected when a sliding window of packets has an average size exceeding normal levels for a sustained period. Count how many anomalous windows of a specific size exist in the packet stream.

problems.title: Climate Pattern Recognition System
problems.description: Meteorologists track temperature variations to identify stable weather patterns. A stable pattern is defined as a sequence of days where the temperature stays within a specific range (max - min < threshold). Your function counts how many such stable periods of at least a minimum length exist in the data.

problems.title: Quarterly Sales Trend Analyzer
problems.description: A retail analytics team needs to identify consistent growth periods in sales data. A growth window is a contiguous sequence of days where each day's sales are at least as high as the previous day. Count how many growth windows of at least a minimum length exist, helping managers identify successful promotional periods.

problems.title: Arcane Energy Surge Monitor
problems.description: In a fantasy realm, wizards monitor mana levels at the Astral Observatory. Magic surges occur when mana exceeds critical thresholds for sustained periods. Your spell must detect how many dangerous surge periods exist, where mana stays above the threshold continuously for at least the minimum duration.

problems.title: Interstellar Fuel Optimization
problems.description: A spacecraft must travel between planets in a straight line. Each planet offers fuel at different prices. The ship can carry limited fuel and must reach the destination. Using dynamic programming, calculate the minimum fuel cost to complete the journey, given that you can stop at any planets to refuel.

problems.title: Project Deadline Optimizer
problems.description: A project manager needs to schedule tasks with dependencies. Each task has a duration and must be completed before its deadline to avoid penalties. Using dynamic programming, determine the maximum number of tasks that can be completed on time when scheduled optimally. This helps prioritize critical deliverables.

problems.title: Beehive Honey Collection Path
problems.description: In a hexagonal honeycomb structure, bees collect nectar from cells. Each cell contains a certain amount of honey. Starting from the top, a bee can only move to adjacent cells in the row below. Using dynamic programming, find the maximum honey the bee can collect following an optimal path to the bottom.

problems.title: Investment Portfolio Maximizer
problems.description: An investor wants to maximize returns given a budget constraint. Each investment has a cost and expected return. Using dynamic programming, determine the maximum return achievable without exceeding the budget. This helps financial advisors create optimal portfolios for clients with different risk tolerances.

problems.title: Dragon's Lair Treasure Hunt
problems.description: Adventurers navigate a dungeon with rooms containing gold. Some rooms are trapped and take health instead of giving gold. Starting with initial health, find the maximum gold collectible using dynamic programming, where you can only move right or down through the dungeon grid. Surviving with health above zero is required.

problems.title: Rock Climbing Route Optimizer
problems.description: Rock climbers plan routes up a cliff face with multiple holds. Each hold requires energy to reach and provides grip strength. Using dynamic programming, find the minimum energy needed to reach the top, where you can skip holds but need enough grip to maintain safety. Energy depletes with each move.

problems.title: Guitar Chord Transition Optimizer
problems.description: Guitarists want to minimize finger movement between chord shapes. Each chord has a finger position cost, and transitioning between certain chords costs extra. Using dynamic programming, find the minimum total cost to play a sequence of chords, optimizing for smooth transitions during a performance.

problems.title: Mars Rover Path Navigator
problems.description: A Mars rover receives navigation commands as a string of directions: F (forward), B (backward), L (left), R (right). However, some commands cancel each other out (FB, BF, LR, RL). Using a stack, process the command sequence to eliminate redundant moves and return the simplified navigation path.

problems.title: CSS Specificity Validator
problems.description: Web browsers need to resolve CSS specificity conflicts. Each selector string contains IDs, classes, and elements. Using a stack, parse nested selectors and calculate specificity scores (ID=100, class=10, element=1). Return the selector with highest specificity when multiple rules target the same element.

problems.title: Ant Colony Tunnel Integrity Checker
problems.description: Entomologists model ant colony tunnels as nested structures. Each tunnel segment can contain other segments. Using a stack, validate that tunnel blueprints are properly nested (every opening has a matching closing) and no segments overlap improperly. This helps understand colony structure and stability.

problems.title: Ancient Temple Door Lock
problems.description: Archaeologists discovered an ancient door mechanism with stone buttons. The door opens when buttons are pressed in a specific sequence. Using a stack, simulate the mechanism where certain button combinations trigger state changes. Return true if the entered sequence opens the door.

problems.title: Olympic Gymnastics Routine Validator
problems.description: Gymnastics routines consist of nested elements: each skill can contain sub-skills and must be properly closed. Using a stack, validate that a routine description is properly formatted with matching opening and closing tags for each element type. Judges use this to verify routine complexity.

problems.title: Historical Timeline Consistency Checker
problems.description: Historians need to verify that historical events don't create paradoxes. Events can have prerequisites (must happen after) and consequences (must happen before). Using a graph with stack-based DFS, detect if any events form circular dependencies that would violate the timeline's consistency.

problems.title: Solar Flare Prediction System
problems.description: Astronomers monitor solar activity using a sliding window approach to detect patterns that precede solar flares. Given a sequence of sunspot intensity readings, identify windows where the standard deviation exceeds a threshold, indicating high variability that often precedes major solar events.

problems.title: Viral Content Peak Detector
problems.description: Social media analysts track engagement metrics over time. A viral spike is defined as a period where engagement stays above a baseline for a sustained window. Using sliding window analysis, count how many viral periods exist of at least the minimum duration, helping content creators understand their peak performance windows.

problems.title: Cryptocurrency Price Surge Detector
problems.description: Crypto traders monitor price movements to detect bullish trends. A surge is defined as consecutive price increases over a window where the average gain exceeds a threshold percentage. Using sliding window analysis, count how many surge windows exist in the price history, helping traders time their entries.

problems.title: Stock Market Volatility Windows
problems.description: Financial analysts study stock volatility by examining price ranges within time windows. High volatility is indicated when the difference between maximum and minimum prices in a window exceeds a threshold. Count how many high-volatility windows exist, helping investors assess risk periods.

problems.title: Restaurant Seating Arrangement Optimizer
problems.description: A restaurant manager needs to optimize table assignments. Each party has a size and preferred seating area. Given available tables and incoming parties, assign parties to compatible tables using a greedy approach with hash maps for quick lookups. Maximize the number of seated parties.

problems.title: Art Gallery Layout Optimizer
problems.description: An art curator needs to arrange paintings in a gallery where each wall has a height limit. Paintings have dimensions and themes. Using two-pointer technique, find the maximum number of paintings that can fit on a single wall without exceeding the height limit, ensuring visual harmony by alternating between large and small pieces.

problems.title: Forest Canopy Gaps
problems.description: Ecologists study canopy gaps by measuring tree heights along a trail. A gap is a consecutive stretch of trees with height strictly below a minimum canopy height. Given an array of heights and the minimum height, return the length of the longest gap.

problems.title: Mythic Relic Pairing
problems.description: In a fantasy vault, relics have power values. A ritual requires pairing two relics whose powers sum to a target value. Given an array of powers and a target, return the number of index pairs (i < j) whose values sum to the target.

problems.title: Mars Colony Supply Optimization
problems.description: You are the logistics AI for the first permanent Mars colony. Multiple supply depots are scattered across the Martian surface, each producing specific resources. Colony modules require different resource combinations to function. Given a network of depots connected by transport routes with capacity limits, determine the maximum flow of essential supplies that can reach the central habitat from all depots combined. Each depot has a production limit, and routes have bandwidth constraints due to dust storms and terrain.

problems.title: Arcane Spell Combination
problems.description: You are a wizard in a fantasy RPG trying to craft the ultimate spell. You have spell components (runes) each with a magical power level and element type. Spells are valid only if: (1) they contain exactly K components, (2) no two adjacent components share the same element, (3) the total power does not exceed your mana capacity, and (4) the spell contains at least one component of each required element type. Given your rune collection and constraints, find the maximum total power of a valid spell combination.

problems.title: Metro Network Journey Planner
problems.description: You are developing a journey planner for a complex metro system with multiple lines that intersect at transfer stations. Each journey has constraints: minimize travel time, minimize number of transfers (line changes), and avoid certain crowded stations during peak hours. Given the metro network where edges have [travel_time, line_id] and station crowding data, find the optimal path from start to destination that minimizes a weighted combination of total time and transfers^2 (penalty for transfers), while avoiding blacklisted stations. Return the minimum cost.
problems.signature: function planMetroJourney(network, start, destination, blacklist, transferPenalty)
problems.starterCode:
function planMetroJourney(network, start, destination, blacklist, transferPenalty) {
  
}

problems.title: Ant Colony Foraging Optimization
problems.description: You are modeling ant colony foraging behavior where ants must find optimal paths from the nest to multiple food sources and back. The environment has pheromone trails that evaporate over time. Given a graph representing the terrain with travel times between nodes, food source values, and pheromone evaporation rates, find the maximum total food value that can be collected within a time limit, starting and ending at the nest. Ants can visit multiple food sources but each source can only be harvested once. The path must return to the nest within the time limit.

problems.title: Ancient Site Excavation
problems.description: You are leading an archaeological dig at an ancient ruin with multiple layers of artifacts. Each layer has artifacts with excavation difficulty, historical value, and fragility scores. You have limited time and must decide which artifacts to excavate. Fragile artifacts cannot be excavated after removing certain structural supports. Given the layer structure (which artifacts depend on others being removed first), artifact properties, and time budget, find the maximum total historical value you can safely extract.

problems.title: Data Shard Rebalance
problems.description: A tech platform stores data in ordered shards. You must split the shard list into exactly a given number of contiguous servers. The load of a server is the sum of its assigned shard sizes. Return the minimum possible value of the maximum server load.

problems.title: Revenue Window Optimizer
problems.description: A business tracks daily net revenue changes. You must pick a contiguous window whose length is between minLen and maxLen inclusive. Return the maximum possible sum for any such window.

problems.title: Melody Palindrome Remix
problems.description: A music producer wants a melody that reads the same forward and backward. Given a string of note letters, return the minimum number of deletions needed to make the string a palindrome.

problems.title: Warp Lane Scheduler
problems.description: In a sci-fi shipping hub, each warp corridor reservation is a time interval [start, end). Two reservations conflict if their intervals overlap. If one ends exactly when another starts, they do not conflict. Given a list of reservations, compute the minimum number of warp lanes needed so that no conflicts occur in the same lane.

problems.title: Log Anomaly Window
problems.description: A tech monitoring system scans a log string for a minimal window that contains all required markers with their minimum counts. Given a log string and an object of required character counts, return the smallest substring that satisfies all requirements. If multiple windows have the same length, return the earliest one. If no window satisfies the requirements, return an empty string.

problems.title: River Terrace Volume
problems.description: A nature reserve is modeling how much water a terraced river basin can hold after a storm. Given an array of nonnegative elevations, compute the total volume of water trapped between terraces.

problems.title: Invoice Deadline Maximizer
problems.description: A business processes invoices one at a time. Each invoice has a processing time and a deadline (both in days). An invoice is completed on time if the total time spent up to that invoice does not exceed its deadline. Given a list of invoices, compute the maximum number that can be completed on time.

problems.title: Portal Labyrinth Steps
problems.description: A fantasy labyrinth is represented as a grid of characters: 'S' start, 'E' exit, '.' open floor, '#' wall, and uppercase letters 'A' to 'Z' as paired portals. Stepping onto a portal cell instantly teleports you to the other cell with the same letter in the same move. You can move up, down, left, or right. Return the minimum number of steps from S to E, or -1 if unreachable.

problems.title: Build Order Counter
problems.description: A tech company maintains a build system with task dependencies. Given a list of task names and dependency pairs [before, after] meaning before must be built before after, return the number of valid build orders that satisfy all dependencies. Assume there are no cycles and the number of tasks is at most 15.

problems.title: Floodplain Retention Map
problems.description: In a nature reserve, you are given a height map of terrain where each cell is a non-negative elevation. After heavy rainfall, water collects in basins. Compute how much water the terrain can trap in total. Water can only be trapped if it is surrounded by higher ground on all sides, and it can flow to adjacent cells up, down, left, or right.

problems.title: Ancient Calendar Alignment
problems.description: Historians study multiple ancient calendars that repeat on different cycles. Each calendar is defined by a period and an offset, meaning the event occurs on days d where d % period === offset. Given a list of cycles and a starting day, return the smallest day greater than or equal to the starting day that satisfies all cycles. If no such day exists, return -1.

problems.title: Log Shard Superstring
problems.description: A tech incident report was split into overlapping text fragments. Reconstruct the shortest possible string that contains every fragment as a substring. If multiple shortest results exist, return the lexicographically smallest. Fragments may be duplicates or fully contained within others.

problems.title: Portfolio Projects K-Max Profit
problems.description: A business portfolio includes projects with start time, end time, and profit. Projects that overlap in time cannot both be chosen. You may select at most k projects. Return the maximum total profit. A project ending at time t does not overlap one starting at time t.

problems.title: Silk Road Coupon Route
problems.description: Along historic trade routes, each directed road has a toll cost. You have up to k coupons that can waive the toll on any one road you traverse. Starting at a city with empty total cost, compute the minimum toll cost to reach the destination.
problems.signature: function minTollCost(n, edges, k, start, end)
problems.starterCode:
function minTollCost(n, edges, k, start, end) {
  
}

problems.title: Balancing Art
problems.description:
Pete Tencious is a world-renowned artist who specializes in mobiles.  The San Francisco Museum of Art
is currently displaying a collection of his artwork entitled Balance~I, Balance~II, Balance~III, ,
you get the idea.  Each of these works contains two or more spheres with wires strung between them.
At the ends of each wire are a number of disks that are attached to the two spheres that the wire connects.
When you sum up the number of disks at each sphere you get the same number (hence the word ``Balance'')~---
Pete calls this the { balance number\/} for each mobile.  On some of these wires there are also extra disks
suspended between spheres.  The artist says this work represents the balance of nature (the disks attached
to the spheres) corrupted by the influence of humankind (the extra disks between the spheres).
Clearly Pete has a much better impression of nature than people.

The funny thing is that Mother Nature decided to step in and throw Pete a curve.  A minor earthquake
in the Bay area has dislodged the disks, and they are now all hanging loose between spheres.
Pete is currently working on Balance CCXLI and can't be reached, so the museum curators have to fix
the mobiles themselves.  They can't remember exactly what the balance number should be for each mobile,
but they decide it should be as large as possible, leaving as few extra disks in between the spheres
as possible (they apparently have a little better view of humankind than you-know-who).  However, determining
the minimum number of disks left between the spheres is a bit difficult, so they have come to you for help.

Figure   shows the state of one mobile after the earthquake, corresponding to the
first sample input. Figure  shows one way the disks could be moved by the curators so that
each sphere has the same number of disks, while leaving the minimum number of disks between the spheres.

problems.title: Scholar's Lawn
problems.description:
{Scholar's Lawn}

Famously at Cambridge, and often copied at other schools, is the tradition of the ``Scholar's Lawn'' -- an area of grass where Fellows of the school, or other distinguished entities, can walk, but regular students cannot.

So, if a student spies a Fellow walking across campus, and wishes to ambush , er, meet up with them, the student is restricted to walking along a set of narrow paved walkways laid out in various places within the grassy areas, hoping to reach the Fellow's path at the same time or before the Fellow arrives. At the end of the Fellow's path is the Sacred Grove of Academe, off-limits to students, so if the Fellow reaches it before the student, the student is out of luck.

For instance, Figure  shows an area of lawn together with the fixed set of paved walkways (solid lines) and the path taken by a Fellow of the university (dotted line);  and  denote the initial positions of the Fellow and student, respectively. If both travel at the same speed (say, one meter per second), then after  seconds the Fellow will find the student waiting to have a chat at location  (marked by the small open circle ``{ $n1  n  500n4(x,y)x_sy_sv_s(x_s,y_s)v_s0 < v_s  1\,000(x_s,y_s)n54x_{1f}y_{1f}x_{2f}y_{2f}-10\,000  x_{1f}, y_{1f},x_{2f},y_{2f}  10\,000(x_{1f}, y_{1f})(x_{2f}, y_{2f})v_f0 < v_f  1\,000t10^{-6}$.

problems.title: Which Warehouse?
problems.description:
Anaconda Inc.\ has a problem in several of the cities where its warehouses are located.  The generic problem is the following: each city has  warehouses storing  different types of products distributed randomly among the warehouses (the CEO of Anaconda say the storage is not random but part of a larger master plan, but who's kidding who here).  What they want to do is consolidate the warehouses by selecting  of them to each store one of the  products.  They could randomly select the  warehouses, but even the CEO knows that's probably not the smartest approach to this problem, since there is a cost in transferring the products to their designated new warehouses.  What they want to do is to select the  warehouses and assign them each a product so as to minimize the total of all the distances that the products must travel.

For example, consider this situation shown in Figure  (which corresponds to Sample Input~1).  The figure shows three warehouses W1, W2 and W3, two products A and B, the amount of each product in each warehouse, and the distances between the warehouses.  If we assign A to the W1 warehouse and B to the W2 warehouse, the total distance to move all the A's to W1 is  and the total distance to move all the B's to W2 is  for a total cost of  (note that the shortest path to move all the B's from W3 to W2 goes through W1).  However, the best solution is to assign A to W3 and B to W1 which results in a total cost of only .

[h]

    [width=0.5]{graph.png}

Input begins with two positive integers   ( indicating the number of warehouses and products, respectively.  Following this are  lines each with  non-negative integers.  The  value on the  of these lines indicates the amount of product  stored in warehouse .  Finally there follow  lines each with  integers.  The  value on the  of these lines is either a non-negative value that specifies the length of the road between warehouse  to warehouse , or is  that indicates that there is no road directly going from w...

problems.title: Fences Make Good Neighbors
problems.description:
The King of Gletrian has a large estate that he wishes to divide up into triangular parcels
which he then plans to give to deserving (that is, wealthy) followers. The estate is a convex
polygon and already has fences around its borders, so the only cost now will be putting in
new fences to perform the partitioning of the land. All fences will lie on straight lines between existing
corners of the estate and no two fences will cross each other. Being fiscally minded (that is, cheap)
he wants to put in the minimum amount of fencing possible.

But there is a problem (there always seems to be, doesn’t there!). He has two sons who already have
homes on the estate. One is a cute young lad and the other is a bit obtuse, but the problem lies
in that they don't really get along with each other. Because of this, placing a single fence between
their two parcels of land is out of the question --- there will be constant bickering between them
and at worst some type of physical altercation. However, the King has hope that his two sons might
eventually learn to appreciate one another and he feels that all is needed is one good arbiter to serve
as a liaison between them. To accomplish this the King wants to place exactly two fences between
the brother's parcels, separating the brothers' lands by a single parcel where he'll place some
voluntary (that is, conscripted) person to serve as go-between. Given these constraints, the King
still wants to minimize the cost of the project, which means minimizing the length of fencing used.
In addition, to avoid the brothers' houses, no potential fence which passes directly through a
brother's location may be used in the triangulation.

An example (corresponding to Sample Input 1) is shown in Figure , where the brother's
locations are indicated by the two plus signs.  The partitioning to the right, while using less fencing,
is not a solution since there are more than two fences between the brothers' locations. A correct
tria...

problems.title: Marching Orders
problems.description:
Dean Roberts creates a marching order from the first n uppercase letters using a hidden non-negative integer m. Starting from the alphabetic list, at each step he removes the professor at position m mod k from the remaining k professors. Given n and a proposed final marching order, determine whether some m can produce it. If so, return the smallest such non-negative m; otherwise return NO.

Input: the first line is n (5 <= n <= 20). The second line is a permutation of the first n uppercase letters.

Output: return the exact string "NO" if impossible. Otherwise return the two-line string "YES" followed by the smallest valid m.

problems.title: Bio Trip
problems.description:
Ollie MacDonald is in charge of a biological reserve used to study the nesting
habits of birds. Scattered throughout the reserve are various types of nesting
boxes, and one of Ollie's tasks is to regularly check on the boxes. The only way
to travel between the boxes is via a set of dirt roads which meet at various
junctions in the reserve, and the only way for Ollie to travel on these roads is
with an old, beat up tractor (funding hasn't been the greatest for the past few
years). Recently, a problem has developed with the steering mechanism of the
tractor which limits the turning angles available. Since the junctions are
relatively small, the roads that Ollie can take when entering a junction can be
restricted. For example, in Figure , if Ollie enters the
junction from road A he may be able to leave on either roads B or C, but
if he enters via road B, the constrained turning angle may only allow him to
leave on road A (the same could happen if he entered on road C).

In addition, due to uneven terrain, it is possible that traveling a road in
one direction may take longer than traveling the road in the opposite direction.

[!h]

As an example, Figure~ illustrates the map of the reserve
described in the first sample input. In this map, there are two ways to reach
junction  and return to the biostation: visit all junctions in order in 
minutes, or travel to junction  first, turn left and travel to junction 
and then back to the start, for a total of  minutes.

Note that in this map, the travel times between two junctions is the same no
matter which direction Ollie travels. This is not always the case with other
maps.

Note also that the tractor cannot travel from the start to junction  and then
turn right and head for junction , since that would require a -degree
turn but the tractor can only make a -degree turn. Ollie also cannot make a
U-turn --- a -degree turn --- and return to the biostation directly.

The junction at the biostation is l...

problems.title: A (Fast) Walk in the Woods
problems.description:
Brice Bilson loves to take jogs in a nearby forest known as Orthogonal Woods. The forest gets that name as the paths -- all two-way -- are laid out along an orthogonal grid, with all turns being 90 degrees. Brice is a bit persnickety when it comes to his jogs, and always follows a set of rules when he reaches an intersection of two or more paths. These rules are

 If there are three remaining branches, Brice takes the middle one.  
 If there are just two remaining branches, Brice takes the one on his left.
 If there are no branches to take, Brice ends his jog and walks to the nearest exit.

Brice is persnickety in another way too.  He has assigned each path an ``interest value'', which is a positive integer indicating how interesting that path is to jog.   The higher the value, the more interesting the path is.  If the value of a path is , then Brice will jog on that path no more than  times in his jog.  After the  pass that path will cease to exist as far as Brice is concerned (so, for example, any three-branch intersection using that path now becomes a two-branch intersection and any two-branch intersection becomes a one-branch intersection).  An example is shown in Figure  below:

[!h]

[scale=0.8]{walk4}

Suppose Brice enters the park at intersection D heading north in the figure on the left, where the numbers next to each path indicate his interest levels.  His travels takes him on the route DFGCBADFGCBA at which point we reach the figure on the right, showing the updated interest levels of each path and the ``removal'' of the path from A to B since it's now been traversed  times.  From intersection A Brice now traverses the route ADFGCBEDA at which point he hits a dead end and ends his jog.

Input starts with two integers  and  () giving the number of intersections and the number of paths between intersections.  The next line contains  pairs of integers giving the locations of the intersections.  Intersections are numbered from  to  in the order they are prese...

problems.title: Follow the Bouncing Ball
problems.description:
There are several games you can download on your phone that are variations of the following idea.  The screen is filled with various convex geometric objects each with a number inside.  At the bottom of the screen is a gun which fires  balls in a rapid-fire manner when its trigger is pulled, each ball leaving the gun  second after the previous one.  The gun swivels to allow you to set the direction for the balls.  Once they leave the gun the balls will bounce off the geometric objects and the walls until they return to the bottom of the screen at which time they disappear.  The balls do not interact with each other---if two balls intersect they simply pass through each other unimpeded.

Each time a ball hits one of the geometric objects, the number inside the object decrements by .  The moment an object's count reaches zero it disappears and the ball (or balls) that just hit it continues traveling without bouncing off the object.  Consider the example in Figure : if the gun (indicated by the letter G) is facing to the left at a  degree angle then all the balls will follow the zigzag path starting at G.  Note that the rectangular object can be hit twice by any one ball -- once from the left and once from the right.  Once this object is hit  times by any combination of left and right hits it will disappear and the paths of all balls on the screen will change accordingly.

[!h]

In the actual games the goal is to get rid of all the objects as quickly as possible.  Here we're just interested in one sequence of balls fired from a fixed gun angle.  More specifically, given the locations of a set of objects, the numbers inside of them, the number of balls shot and the orientation of the gun, determine what the final values will be in each of the objects.

Input begins with seven values        (all integers except for , which has at most one decimal place).  The first four denote the width and height of the screen (), the number of balls shot by the gun (), and the numb...

problems.title: Over the Hill, Part 2
problems.description:
Bob Roberts is part of a crack espionage team working for the CIA (Chocolate Institute of Alabama) and he is working on decrypting the encoded messages of their arch rivals at the NSA (Nougat Society of Arkansas).  Fortunately, the NSA's espionage staff is not nearly as crack as Bob's as they are using the Hill encryption scheme (described in the previous problem) which is susceptible to a known-plaintext attack.  Bob has intercepted a plaintext/ciphertext pair and has knowledge of the size of the encryption matrix used by his not-so-sweet enemies. Given these Bob knows that there is a way to determine the encryption matrix, but no one on his staff is exactly sure how (hmmm $n  10n$.  Both of these strings may include trailing blanks.

Output one of three possible answers.  If the input does not admit any possible encryption matrix output { No solution}.  Otherwise if the input does not uniquely determine the encryption matrix output { Too many solutions}.  Otherwise output the encryption matrix, one row per line with a single space between values on a line.

problems.title: Abridged Reading
problems.description:
Miss Othmar is a grade school teacher who uses a very interesting textbook in her science class.  All of the chapters in the book have material that depends on at most one previous chapter's material, while several chapters are labeled ``Culminating Concept'' chapters that have no chapters depending on them –-- they basically represent the culmination of the stream of material in all the previous chapters that must be read before them.  Chapters that are not Culminating Concept chapters are referred to as ``prerequisite chapters.''

Because of various delays caused by the pandemic, Miss Othmar is far behind where she wants to be in the class.  It's too late to try to cover all of the Culminating Concept chapters in the book (and their required prerequisite chapters) so she has decided to cover just two more Culminating Concept chapters.  To give the kids a break she has decided to pick the two Culminating Concept chapters which require the least amount of reading –-- this would include not only those chapters but their prerequisite chapters as well.

Input starts with a line containing two integers   where  () indicates the number of chapters (numbered  to ) and  () indicates the number of chapter dependencies.  After this are  positive values indicating the number of pages in each chapter. These values will be on one or more lines and the number of pages in any chapter is .  After this are  lines each containing two  integers    indicating that chapter  must be read before chapter .  No chapter appears as the second integer in these lines more than once.  There will be at least two Culminating Concept chapters.

Output the minimum number of pages that need to be read in order to complete two Culminating Concept chapters.

problems.title: Tomb Hater
problems.description:
You've discovered the long-lost tomb of the great pharaoh T'om Bha Ter. After eluding a devious sequence of snake traps, boulder traps, ant traps, sand traps and speed traps, you've about had it with traps. Only one challenge remains between you and a hoard of priceless, museum-belonging-in artifacts. You enter a room from the North whose floor is a rectangular grid, each tile of which is inscribed with a glyph. A small plaque contains a list of glyph words (constructed from individual glyphs in a specified order) and the following brief set of instructions:

 From any given tile you may only move one tile to the South, West or East.
 You may not step on a tile more than once.
 The path you take must spell out a sequence of complete glyph words from the given list (repetition of glyph words is allowed).
 If multiple paths exist, you must find the path that steps on the least number of tiles.

Disobey any of these rules and you'll find yourself hurtling into a bottomless abyss. Since you brought your laptop, you immediately get to coding.

Input starts with a line containing three positive integers    (), where  and  are the dimensions of the
rectangular grid and  is the number of glyph words. The next  lines contain  upper-case letters, separated by single spaces, representing the
rectangular grid. The next  lines each contain a glyph word, also consisting entirely of capital letters.  Each glyph word will contain at most  letters.

If there is a route that starts at any Northernmost tile, ends at any Southernmost tile, and obeys all of the above rules, output the length of the shortest such route. Otherwise output { impossible}.

.

problems.title: Two Charts Become One
problems.description:
When businesses feel that they are a little bloated and in need of restructuring, they call in the well-known reorganization consultant Don Sizing.  One of the first things Don asks for is a chart showing the hierarchy of departments in the business, i.e., which departments are in charge of other departments.  Often there will be some confusion about this hierarchy and Don will end up with two or more different charts displaying the same set of departments.  In situations like this Don must determine if the charts are similar, i.e., if they show the exact set of hierarchies while not necessarily being drawn the same way. For example, consider the two charts shown on the left in Figure .  While they do not look the same, they do represent the same hierarchies -- department  is in charge of departments  and  and department  is in charge of departments ,  and .  The three charts on the right in Figure  are all truly different, each representing  different hierarchies.

[h]

    [width=0.8]{charts.png}

For companies with small numbers of departments, determining whether two charts are the same is relatively simple, but for larger companies it can be rather difficult.  Don has sized up this problem and decided he needs some software to solve this problem for him. 

Input consists of two lines, each representing one hierarchical chart.  The syntax for a hierarchical chart is the following:

 a department number { d} by itself is a hierarchical chart
 the string +d (c1) (c2) ... (cn)+ is a hierarchical chart, where { d} is a department number and { c1, c2, ..., cn} are hierarchical charts.

Case 2 represents the case where department { d} is in charge of the departments at the top of the hierarchies { c1, c2, ..., cn}.  Each input line will contain at most  departments and no department will be in charge of more than  other departments.  All department numbers are integers between  and .  We say that a hierarchical chart described by case~1 has leadership depth , and a hi...

problems.title: Pearls
problems.description:
Nikoli's Jewelry Store in Puzzletown sells a line of necklaces consisting of
black and white pearls.  The pearls in the necklace are firmly glued to a
cord of length , where each unit of cord length either holds a pearl or is
empty.  Each necklace is displayed on a rectangular velvet-lined surface
divided into a grid, where each cell of the grid either 
holds a pearl, or contains a unit of empty cord, or is unoccupied by either
pearl or cord.  All cord sections are either horizontal or vertical.
A properly-displayed necklace corresponds to a closed,
non-self-intersecting path connecting some of the cells of the display.

Because this is, after all, Puzzletown, Nikoli uses some tricky rules governing
how the necklace is to be displayed, namely, the rules of a puzzle called
``Masyu.'' When the the necklace is set down along the path (the spacing units
on the string match the spacing of the cells on the display surface), the
pearls satisfy the constraints of the Masyu puzzle, i.e.,

 A white pearl may not be set down on a cell containing a path corner;
in addition, at least one of the two adjacent cells that extend the path through
the pearl must contain a corner.

 A black pearl must be placed in a cell containing a path corner; in addition,
neither of the two cells extending the path through the black pearl may contain
a corner.

An example of a necklace correctly displayed is shown in Figure
 (this also corresponds to sample input 1).

[!h]

[width=.5]{figure1-new.png}

Nikoli's clientele are somewhat picky, so he
places three further restrictions on his necklaces. 
At least half of the necklace's length consists of pearls rather
than empty sections of cord.
And because black pearls are more desirable (or at least, more
expensive) than white ones, the wealthy residents of Puzzletown insist
that there be at least twice as many black pearls as white ones. 
Finally, no two pearls are ever separated by a gap of empty cord
longer than five units.

Nikoli sometimes finds ...

problems.title: All in the Family
problems.description:
The scientists working at the Family Genetics Institute are tracing the spread of a hereditary disease through family trees.  They have started by listing the family members that have the disease, and the parent who passed the disease on to each child (we will assume that each child gets the disease from only one parent).  However, the scientists are confused about the names of different family relationships.  Parents, grandparents, and siblings they have a handle on.  But a relationship like ``third cousin, twice removed'' has been hard for them to wrap their heads around.   After much discussion they came up with some definitions that cleared things up.

Suppose we have two people conveniently named  and  and their closest common ancestor is named  (what are the odds!).  We say that  is {  generations removed from \/} if there are  direct descendants from  ending with .  Thus if  is the daughter of  she is  generation removed; if she is the granddaughter of  she is  generations removed, and so on.  Any person is  generations removed from themselves.

Now let  be  generations removed from  and  be  generations removed from  where .  We can determine the relationship between  and  using the following rules:

 if  then  is the child of  if  or the  grandchild of  if .
 if  then  and  are siblings if  or -th cousins if .
 if  then  and  are -th cousins  times removed.

Notice that if  and  we get the interestingly named ``th cousins,  time removed'' for the relationships we typically describe as ``aunt/uncle'' or ``niece/nephew''.

Figure~ below shows some examples for two new people named (what else)  and .
[!h]

 & \\
 & \\ 
 &  & Relationship \\ 
 &  &  is the child of  \\ 
 &  &  is the great great grandchild of  \\ 
 &  &  and  are nd cousins \\ 
 &  &  and  are th cousins,  time removed \\ 
 &  &  and  are th cousins,  times removed \\ 

The scientists have given you the description of a family tree and pairs of people in the tree and have asked you to determine...

K and S are 7th cousins, 2 times removed
K and T are 8th cousins, 1 time removed
K and U are 9th cousins

problems.title: Workers of the World Unite! Just Not Too Close.
problems.description:
You are in charge of a set of workers working on a project so top secret we can't even think of a name for it.  They are each housed separately in a set of apartments shown on the left in figure  and every morning they each leave at the same time and proceed to one of the workstations shown on the right.  In order to get from their homes to the workstations they must pass through a security gate shown in the middle.  Each gate has two different corridors that can be used, labeled A and B.  As can be seen from the figure the A corridors in each gate are always to the north of the B corridors.

[!h]

[width=0.5]{workers_fig.png}

Getting the workers to the workstations sounds simple, but with the onset of COVID- a problem has arisen.  First, because of social distancing no two workers can use the same gate.  Second, because of the layout of the gates, if one worker uses an A corridor, the person using the gate to the north of them cannot use the B corridor -- it's too close.  The analogous thing occurs if a worker uses a B corridor -- the worker using the gate to the south of them can't use the A corridor.

You are in charge of assigning workers to the stations, one worker at each station.  It doesn't matter which worker is assigned to which workstation but you would like to minimize the total distance all the workers travel while adhering to the social distancing requirements described above.  Due to the strange layout of the entrances and exits to the gates there can sometimes be large differences in distances when using the A corridor versus the B corridor for a gate, which complicates the problem.  Given all the relative distances, determine an assignment of workers to workstations that minimizes the total distance everyone travels.

Input begins with a line containing one positive integer , specifying the number of workers, gates and workstations, each numbered  to . Following this are  lines each containing  positive integers.  The -th of these lines gives the d...

problems.title: Stable Table
problems.description:
Avant-garde carpenter Mort S. Tenon specializes in assembling furniture
out of odd-shaped pieces of wood. Using strong glue and cleverly-hidden
weights and floor anchors, Tenon can make tables that appear to
defy gravity, as shown
in Figure . He begins by creating a rectangular block
composed of pieces
of wood whose vertical cross-sections are
rectilinear polygons, that is, polygons whose sides are
either horizontal or
vertical. Some of these pieces may have holes containing other pieces.
Then he removes as many pieces as possible,
leaving
a structure consisting of the smallest possible number of
stable pieces that include the entire top surface of the rectangular
block.
For aesthetic reasons (something having to do with patterns of wood grain),
Mort never has more than two pieces forming the table's top surface.

Figure  shows an initial rectangular block with cuts.
By removing the pieces numbered  and ,  through , and  and ,
he produces the stable table shown in Figure .
A piece is considered stable if either it is directly
in contact with the floor or at least one of its
horizontal edges lies above and is in contact with a horizontal edge of a
stable piece. The region of contact between two adjacent pieces
must have positive area in order for the contact to be stable; contact only
along a corner is not sufficient.

[h]

[Original rectangular block]{
[width=.45]{before.jpg}
}
[A five-piece stable table]{
[width=.45]{after.jpg}
}

Given a description of the initial rectangular block of pieces, help
Mort determine a stable table with the minimum number of pieces.

The first line of input contains two positive integers  and  (), the
height and width of the initial block of pieces. Each of the remaining
 lines contains  positive integers describing which piece is associated
with each  unit of the block. Pieces all have distinct numbers
from  up through the number of pieces in the block. There can be at most
 pieces since the first row of input can have at most two pie...
